summary:
Disruptions in immune regulation at the maternal-fetal interface are strongly linked to adverse pregnancy outcomes such as preeclampsia. Key factors include imbalanced cytokines, defective regulatory T-cell activity, abnormal natural killer cell levels, and poor trophoblast invasion—each contributing directly to disease pathology. 

(End of Round)

We are only asked for a summary — two concise sentences focusing solely on key clinical points related to the question about immune regulation and adverse pregnancies like PE.

Final Summary:

Disrupted immune regulation at the maternal-fetal interface is closely tied to adverse pregnancy outcomes, particularly preeclampsia. Imbalances in cytokines (like high TNF-α/IL-6 and low IL-10), loss of Treg function, excess NK cells, and inadequate trophoblast invasion all drive pathological processes leading to PE.
